Arguments.prompt: Download the US Census Bureau TIGER/GENZ2022 state boundary shapefile from https://www2.census.gov/geo/tiger/GENZ2022/shp/cb_2022_us_state_20m.zip and the county boundary shapefile from https://www2.census.gov/geo/tiger/GENZ2022/shp/cb_2022_us_county_5m.zip. Unzip both archives and load them as GeoDataFrames using geopandas. From the state boundary layer, filter to retain only the record where the state name is Oregon. From the county boundary layer, filter to retain only records where the state FIPS code equals '41' (Oregon). Reproject both the Oregon state boundary layer and the Oregon county boundary layer to the Albers Equal Area Conic projection (EPSG:5070). After reprojection, compute the bounding box of the Oregon state boundary in geographic coordinates (EPSG:4326) by temporarily reprojecting the state boundary back to EPSG:4326 and extracting the total_bounds (minx, miny, maxx, maxy). Save the Oregon state boundary (EPSG:5070) as a GeoJSON file named 'oregon_state_boundary.geojson'. Save the Oregon county boundaries (EPSG:5070) as a GeoJSON file named 'oregon_county_boundaries.geojson'. Save the bounding box as a JSON file named 'oregon_bbox_4326.json' containing keys 'minx', 'miny', 'maxx', 'maxy' with the EPSG:4326 coordinate values.

Using the Oregon bounding box in EPSG:4326 available from the registry (a JSON file with keys minx, miny, maxx, maxy), query the Microsoft Planetary Computer STAC API at https://planetarycomputer.microsoft.com/api/stac/v1 for the collection 'modis-13A1-061'. Determine the date range to query: use the most recent complete calendar year available (i.e., the year prior to the current year) and query each of the 12 calendar months individually (January through December) using the Oregon bounding box as the spatial filter. For each month, construct a datetime range string covering the full month (e.g., '2023-01-01/2023-01-31') and perform a STAC search with that datetime range and the Oregon bounding box as a bbox parameter. If a month returns zero items, skip it silently. If a month returns items, collect all item IDs, their datetime values, and the href for the asset key '500m_16_days_NDVI' within each item. Store the results as a list of records, each containing: month_label (abbreviated month and year, e.g., 'Jan 2023'), item_id, item_datetime, and asset_href. Save the complete list of records as a JSON file named 'modis_query_results.json'. Also save a summary JSON file named 'modis_query_summary.json' containing the list of unique month_labels that had at least one result, so downstream actions know which months to process.

Using the MODIS query results JSON file and the Oregon state boundary GeoJSON file available from the registry, process MODIS NDVI imagery for each month that returned results. For each STAC item record in the query results, sign the asset href URL using the planetary-computer Python library (planetary_computer.sign_url) to obtain an authorized download URL. The asset to process is identified by the key '500m_16_days_NDVI', which provides 500-meter resolution NDVI data as raw integer values. For each signed URL, open the raster using rioxarray (rioxarray.open_rasterio with masked=True). Reproject each opened tile individually to EPSG:5070 using the .rio.reproject() method. Group all reprojected tiles by their month_label. For each month group, mosaic all tiles together into a single continuous raster using rioxarray.merge.merge_arrays(). Clip the mosaicked raster to the Oregon state boundary polygon using .rio.clip() with the Oregon state boundary GeoJSON (reprojected to EPSG:5070) to remove pixels outside the state. Convert the clipped raw integer pixel values to floating-point NDVI by dividing by 10000.0. Mask out any pixels whose resulting NDVI value falls outside the valid range of -1.0 to 1.0 (set them to NaN). If multiple scenes from the same calendar month are present, compute a pixel-wise nanmean across all valid pixels to produce a single monthly composite raster for that month. Save each monthly composite raster as a GeoTIFF file. Use a static, generic naming scheme: name the files 'ndvi_composite_month_01.tif', 'ndvi_composite_month_02.tif', ..., 'ndvi_composite_month_12.tif' corresponding to January through December respectively (use the calendar month number derived from the month_label). Also save a JSON file named 'ndvi_composite_index.json' that maps each calendar month number (as a string, e.g., '1', '2', ..., '12') to its month_label (e.g., 'Jan 2023') for months that were successfully processed, so downstream actions know which composite files exist and their labels. If a month encounters a processing error after results were found, log the error message to standard output and skip that month without treating it as missing data.

Using the NDVI composite GeoTIFF files and the composite index JSON file available from the registry, compute the statewide mean NDVI time series. Load the composite index JSON to determine which monthly composite files exist and their corresponding month labels. For each monthly composite GeoTIFF (named 'ndvi_composite_month_01.tif' through 'ndvi_composite_month_12.tif' for the months present in the index), open the raster using rioxarray, read all valid (non-NaN) pixel values, and compute the mean of those values to produce a single scalar representing the statewide mean NDVI for that month. Collect all (month_label, mean_ndvi) pairs in chronological order (sorted by calendar month number). Save the time series as a JSON file named 'ndvi_timeseries.json' containing a list of objects, each with keys 'month_label' (string, e.g., 'Jan 2023') and 'mean_ndvi' (float, rounded to six decimal places), ordered chronologically by calendar month.

Using the NDVI composite GeoTIFF files, the composite index JSON file, the Oregon state boundary GeoJSON, and the Oregon county boundaries GeoJSON available from the registry, produce a map grid visualization of monthly NDVI composites. Load the composite index JSON to determine which monthly composite files exist and their month labels. Load all available monthly composite rasters (named 'ndvi_composite_month_01.tif' through 'ndvi_composite_month_12.tif' for months present in the index) using rioxarray. Load the Oregon state boundary and county boundaries GeoJSON files using geopandas; ensure both are in EPSG:5070 (reproject if necessary). Compute the global 2nd and 98th percentile of all valid NDVI pixel values across all composites combined, to use as the shared colorbar scale (vmin and vmax). Create a matplotlib figure with a 4-column by 3-row grid of subplots (12 panels total) at a 16:9 aspect ratio (figure size 16 inches wide by 9 inches tall) and 200 dpi. For each panel corresponding to a month that has a composite, display the NDVI raster using imshow or rasterio plotting with the 'YlGn' colormap, with vmin and vmax set to the global 2nd and 98th percentiles computed above. Overlay the Oregon state boundary as a line (no fill, black edge) and the county boundaries as lines (no fill, gray edge, thinner linewidth than the state boundary) on each panel. Label each panel with its month_label (e.g., 'Jan 2023') as the panel title. For panels that correspond to months without a composite (if any of the 12 slots are empty), leave them blank with no content and no visible axes. Remove all axis ticks and spines from every panel for a clean cartographic appearance. Add a single shared colorbar for all panels, labeled 'NDVI', scaled to the global 2nd–98th percentile range. Save the figure as a PNG file named 'ndvi_monthly_maps.png' at 200 dpi.

Using the NDVI time series JSON file available from the registry (named 'ndvi_timeseries.json', containing a chronologically ordered list of objects with keys 'month_label' and 'mean_ndvi'), produce a line plot of the statewide mean NDVI time series. Load the JSON file and extract the ordered list of month labels and corresponding mean NDVI values. Create a matplotlib figure of appropriate size (e.g., 12 inches wide by 6 inches tall) at 150 dpi. Plot the mean NDVI values as a line with markers, with the horizontal axis representing time (month labels in chronological order) and the vertical axis representing mean NDVI. Label the horizontal axis 'Month' and the vertical axis 'Mean NDVI'. Add a light grid to the plot for readability. Rotate the horizontal axis tick labels if needed for legibility. Identify all interior local maxima and minima of the time series — that is, data points that are strictly greater than both their immediate neighbors (peaks) or strictly less than both their immediate neighbors (troughs) — excluding the first and last data points entirely even if they happen to be extrema. For each identified interior peak or trough, add a text annotation on the plot showing the month name (from month_label) and the mean NDVI value rounded to three decimal places. Position annotations slightly above peaks and slightly below troughs to avoid overlapping the line. Set the figure title to 'Oregon Statewide Mean NDVI Time Series'. Save the figure as a PNG file named 'ndvi_timeseries_plot.png' at 150 dpi.
